title: Topic 409: Why News Games Exist: Journalism and Interactivity
content:
# Topic 409: Why News Games Exist: Journalism and Interactivity

## ABSTRACT

This paper posits a radical, earth-shattering, and frankly, quite obvious thesis: newsgames exist because reading is hard and clicking buttons is easy. In an era where the average attention span is dwindling faster than an ice cream cone in a heatwave, journalism has been forced to adapt, resulting in the unholy, yet surprisingly effective, matrimony of interactive entertainment and hard-hitting news. This scholarly inquiry delves into the bizarre world of newsgames, exploring how they leverage psychological trickery, from the illusion of choice to the gamification of tragedy, to make us engage with topics we’d otherwise scroll past. We will dissect the mechanics of these digital Trojan horses, analyze their impact on our mushy, media-saturated brains, and question whether we are becoming more informed citizens or just lab rats in a vast, click-based experiment. The conclusion is as inevitable as a software update: the future of news is playable, and it’s probably a lot more fun than it should be.

## INTRODUCTION

It’s 11 PM. You’re in bed, bathed in the pale, apocalyptic glow of your smartphone, doomscrolling through an endless feed of political scandals, climate disasters, and celebrity gossip. Your thumb is sore, your soul is weary, and you’ve retained approximately 0.01% of the information you’ve consumed. This, dear reader, is the modern news experience: a firehose of misery sprayed directly into your eyeballs. But what if there was another way? A better way? Or at least, a more distracting one? Enter the newsgame, the chocolate-covered broccoli of the media world. It’s the news, but with points, levels, and the satisfying illusion of control. Newsgames are the result of a desperate, some might say heroic, attempt by journalists to trick us into learning. They are interactive experiences that simulate real-world events, from navigating the complexities of international diplomacy to understanding the plight of a refugee. The concept is simple: if you can’t beat ‘em, make ‘em play. This paper will embark on a rollicking journey through the history, psychology, and cultural impact of newsgames. We will explore how these interactive oddities came to be, from their humble beginnings as glorified political cartoons to their current status as sophisticated tools of persuasion. We will examine the psychological sleight-of-hand that makes them so compelling, and we will ask the all-important question: are newsgames the future of journalism, or just a passing fad in our never-ending quest for digital distraction? Fasten your seatbelts, folks. It’s going to be a bumpy, but hopefully, educational ride.

## LITERATURE REVIEW

The academic world, in its infinite wisdom, has not been entirely silent on the peculiar phenomenon of newsgames. Scholars, presumably after getting tired of writing papers about Shakespeare or the mating habits of the fruit fly, have turned their attention to this digital curiosity. The consensus, if one can be found in the cacophony of academic discourse, is that newsgames are a 'hybrid' of journalism and gaming [1]. A stunning revelation, I know. It’s like discovering that a spork is a hybrid of a spoon and a fork. 

The godfather of newsgame studies is arguably Ian Bogost, who, along with his colleagues, defined newsgames as a 'body of work produced at the intersection of video games and journalism' [2]. Bogost also gifted us with the term 'procedural rhetoric,' which is a fancy way of saying that games persuade us not through words or images, but through their rules and processes [3]. In a newsgame, you don’t just read about the soul-crushing bureaucracy of the immigration system; you experience it by filling out endless virtual forms and waiting in interminable digital queues. It’s a form of empathy-building so effective it should probably be illegal.

The history of newsgames is as long and storied as a CVS receipt. The genre is often traced back to Gonzalo Frasca’s *September 12*, a game that lets you bomb a Middle Eastern village in an attempt to kill a single terrorist, only to find that every civilian you kill turns into another terrorist [4]. It’s a grim, Sisyphean task that perfectly captures the futility of the War on Terror, and it’s about as fun as a root canal. But that’s the point. Newsgames aren’t always meant to be fun. They’re meant to make you think, to feel, to question. And maybe, just maybe, to click on a few ads along the way.

A 2018 study by Wolf and Godulla found that while media organizations are increasingly producing newsgames, they are 'doing little to exploit the wide range of design options' [5]. The games they produce often receive a 'muted reception' and have little positive effect on the media brand. This suggests that the news industry is still in the experimental phase, throwing digital spaghetti at the wall to see what sticks. Some of it, as we will see, is stickier than others.

Further delving into the academic abyss, we find that the conversation around newsgames is not just about their existence, but their very nature and purpose. Some scholars, with the earnestness of a golden retriever, argue that newsgames are a powerful tool for civic engagement, a way to make the public more informed and more involved in the democratic process. Others, with the cynicism of a world-weary detective, see them as a Trojan horse for corporate and political interests, a way to subtly shape public opinion while we’re busy chasing high scores. The truth, as is often the case, is probably a messy combination of both. The field of “persuasive games” has grown alongside newsgames, with researchers exploring how interactive systems can be designed to influence players' beliefs and behaviors. This adds another layer of complexity to the discussion, as the line between informing and persuading becomes increasingly blurry. Are newsgames a new form of journalism, or a new form of propaganda? The answer, it seems, depends on who you ask, and what they’re trying to sell you.

## ANALYSIS

So, why do these digital contraptions work? Why are we more likely to engage with a news story if it’s dressed up as a game? The answer, my friends, lies in the squishy, easily manipulated organ between your ears: your brain. Newsgames are masters of psychological manipulation, using a variety of tricks to hook us in and keep us clicking.

First, there’s the **illusion of agency**. Newsgames give us the feeling that we are in control, that our choices matter. In reality, our choices are often limited to a few pre-determined paths, all leading to the same inevitable conclusion. But the illusion is powerful. It makes us feel invested in the outcome, even if that outcome is as bleak as a Russian novel. This is the same principle that makes us feel good about choosing between two equally terrible candidates in an election. We may not like the options, but at least we got to choose.

Then there’s the **gamification of everything**. Newsgames take the mundane and often depressing reality of the news and sprinkle it with points, badges, and leaderboards. Suddenly, learning about the global refugee crisis is no longer a somber exercise in empathy, but a high-stakes game where you can compete with your friends to see who can be the most compassionate. It’s a bit like putting a gold star on a tax return, but it works. Our brains are wired to seek rewards, and newsgames are more than happy to provide them, even if they are as meaningless as a participation trophy.

Finally, there’s the power of **narrative transportation**. Newsgames don’t just tell us a story; they immerse us in it. We are no longer passive observers, but active participants in the unfolding drama. This can lead to a deeper understanding and a more emotional connection to the subject matter. It’s one thing to read an article about the struggles of a minimum-wage worker; it’s another to play a game where you have to make impossible choices between paying rent and buying food. The latter is far more likely to stick with you, haunting your dreams and making you question the very fabric of our capitalist society. Or at least, it will make you think twice before complaining about the price of your artisanal latte.

Let's not forget the role of **cognitive dissonance**, that delightful mental discomfort we experience when holding two conflicting beliefs. Newsgames are masters of inducing this state. You might believe you're a good person, but the game forces you to make morally ambiguous choices with no clear right answer. This creates a tension that your brain is desperate to resolve. You might try to justify your actions, rationalize your choices, or even change your beliefs to align with your in-game behavior. This process of wrestling with cognitive dissonance can lead to a deeper and more nuanced understanding of the issues at play. It's like a philosophical workout for your brain, and the only sweat involved is the cold sweat of existential dread. The beauty of this is that the game doesn't need to preach a specific message; it simply creates the conditions for you to question your own assumptions. It's a subtle but powerful form of persuasion, and it's far more effective than a preachy op-ed. The game becomes a mirror, reflecting our own biases and inconsistencies back at us, forcing us to confront the messy reality of our own moral compass.

## REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES

The world of newsgames is a veritable menagerie of the weird, the wonderful, and the downright bizarre. Let’s take a look at a few notable specimens.

***The New York Times Simulator***: This satirical masterpiece by Paolo Pedercini puts you in the shoes of the editor of *The New York Times*. Your goal is to maximize readership while appeasing the powerful interests of the police, the rich, and the state of Israel. The game is a scathing critique of media bias and the concept of “manufactured consent,” and it’s about as subtle as a sledgehammer. But it’s also hilarious, and it forces players to confront the uncomfortable realities of modern journalism. It’s the kind of game that makes you laugh and then immediately want to go live in a cabin in the woods, far away from the corrupting influence of the media.

***Bad News***: This game, developed by the University of Cambridge, puts you in the role of a fake news tycoon. Your goal is to amass as many followers as possible by spreading misinformation and manipulating public opinion. It’s a brilliant piece of “inoculation theory,” teaching players to spot the techniques of fake news by making them the purveyors of it. It’s also a deeply cynical and depressing experience, but in a fun way. It’s like a flu shot for your brain, but with more memes.

***Darfur is Dying***: This game, created by MTVU, is a more serious take on the newsgame genre. It puts players in the role of a Darfuri refugee, forcing them to navigate the daily struggles of life in a conflict zone. The game is a powerful tool for building empathy, and it has been praised for its ability to raise awareness about the crisis in Darfur. It’s also a stark reminder that not all games are fun, and that some are designed to make you feel uncomfortable, to challenge your assumptions, and to hopefully, inspire you to action.

## DISCUSSION

So, what does the rise of newsgames tell us about ourselves? Are we becoming a society of overgrown children who can only process information if it’s presented in the form of a game? Or are we simply adapting to a new media landscape, one that is more interactive, more immersive, and more demanding of our attention?

The truth, as always, is probably somewhere in the middle. Newsgames are a reflection of our changing relationship with the news. We are no longer content to be passive consumers of information; we want to be active participants. We want to feel like we are part of the story, that our choices matter. Newsgames cater to this desire, offering us a sense of agency and control in a world that often feels chaotic and overwhelming.

But there’s a dark side to all this interactivity. Newsgames can be powerful tools of persuasion, and they can be used to manipulate our emotions and our opinions. The same techniques that can be used to build empathy can also be used to spread propaganda. The line between journalism and entertainment is becoming increasingly blurred, and it’s not always clear where one ends and the other begins. As we move forward into this brave new world of playable news, it’s important to remain critical, to question the motives of the creators, and to remember that just because something is a game, doesn’t mean it’s not serious.

## CONCLUSION

In the end, newsgames are a bit like a first date. They can be awkward, they can be cringey, and they can be a complete waste of time. But they can also be exciting, they can be enlightening, and they can be the start of something beautiful. The future of news is still being written, and it’s likely to be a messy, chaotic, and often contradictory affair. But one thing is for sure: it’s going to be a lot more interactive. And who knows, it might even be fun. Now if you’ll excuse me, I have a high score to beat in the game of global politics. Wish me luck.
references:
[1] Bogost, I., Ferrari, S., & Schweizer, B. (2010). *Newsgames: Journalism at play*. MIT press.

[2] Bogost, I. (2007). *Persuasive games: The expressive power of videogames*. MIT press.

[3] Frasca, G. (2003). *September 12: A toy world*. Newsgaming.com.

[4] Wolf, C., & Godulla, A. (2018). Newsgames in journalism: Exploitation of potential and assessment by recipients. *Journalism Research*, *1*(2), 2-20.

[5] Pedercini, P. (2024). *The New York Times Simulator*. Molleindustria.
